plan.md.text:
Yes — load-bearing correction. My A.4 wording was eager ("variable.set coerces via the registry"); that's wrong. Updated to: variable.set tags Data with .Type and stores the raw value untouched. No parsing at set time. The materializer (from the Serializers registry) runs lazily on first access — `%var.rows%` or `Data.As<Csv>()` triggers it, parses once, caches the typed view on Data for subsequent accesses. Same discipline as Lazy params and IBooleanResolvable's deferred I/O.

One consequence worth flagging: if a declared Type has no materializer, the error surfaces at the first **access** site, not at the set site. I think that's right — the access is where the developer expected the typed shape, so it's where the error makes sense — but it's a small contract decision. Locked unless you want it loud-at-set instead.

Updated plan: decisions table + A.4 + open-note #5.
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.text:
Three parts:

(1) **List<File> stays.** I checked app/tester/File.cs — it carries 9 fields beyond Path (PrPath, EntryGoalName, Status, Directory, Goal, GoalHash, BuilderVersion, Tags, StatusReason). It's a real test-domain object, not a path wrapper. Collapsing to List<path> would lose all that. If at some later point those fields move onto path itself, we'd revisit, but that's a bigger refactor than this branch.

(2) **variable.set as universal converter — adopted.** This is the keystone insight and it collapses a huge chunk of v1. Every action's capture is already `variable.set Value=%!data% Type=<T>`. If variable.set knows how to coerce by Type (json → JsonNode, csv → Csv, xlsx → Workbook), every action becomes type-coercible without the action knowing about types. output.ask, file.read, http.request, llm.query all become 'annotate the trailing variable.set Type' — no per-action infrastructure. New section A.4 is built around this. Section A.5 (cast hint) drops to one paragraph because there's nothing to parse — the LLM reads `%var%(type)` as prompt text and uses it for the Type annotation; the converter on variable.set does the work at runtime.

(3) **MockHandle → Mock.** Renamed. Lives at app/mock/Mock/this.cs per the singular-folder rule; the doubled name is the accepted cost.
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.text: Agreed — `List<Dictionary<string,string>>` was lazy. Plan updated: coder reads the current `Builder.Types(this)` implementation and extracts the actual shape into a strongly-typed record (likely `app/builder/Types/TypeInfo/this.cs` or reuses an existing type model if one fits — there's already an `app/types/` model that may slot in directly). The string-keyed dict goes away.

plan.md.text: llm.query can be figured out by when the user request, because he send format/scheme to llm, when he sends scheme it's json, also format can be '.md' => md, 'csharp' => csharp, etc...
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text: Right — moved out of 'stay polymorphic'. llm.query is determinable from its own args: `schema` set → Type=`json`; `format` arg → that format. The C# signature stays `Data<object>` (the value still is whatever the LLM returns), but the trailing variable.set gets a real Type annotation via the per-action prompt rules in A.4. Only two actions are now genuinely polymorphic at the C# level: `goal.call` (needs cross-goal analysis) and `settings.get` (depends on what was stored).
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.text: no no no no, there is not syntax in plang. this is just a hint to the llm. c# never parses this.
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text: Critical correction accepted. Section A.5 rewritten — it's now one paragraph instead of ~30 lines. There is no new PLang syntax, no schema change, no parser work, no `castType` field. The dev writes `file.read %path%(csv)` as plain step text; the LLM reads it; Compile.llm has one rule ("variable followed by `(type)` is a Type hint for the slot") that tells the LLM to put that type on the trailing variable.set's existing `Type` parameter. PLang's tokenizer never sees `(csv)` — it's free-form prose like any other text in step descriptions. The runtime converter on variable.set (A.4) does the actual work.
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.text: I think this is basically happening on the variable.set, so even though we say '- read file.xlsx', it is always read into %!data% at runtime, that %!data% can have .type='xlsx'. when we build, hmmm.... yeah, then we dont know the type, so it must be up to the file.read module to somehow give that information to the builder. I think we are coming to the part where we need to create a Build method for actions, that runs the build method on each action after it has built. Then we could have Build() in file/read.cs that would parse the filename and propigate the type to the builder. ok, this is another branch, so lets leave it for now.
plan.md.anchor: **Open question.** Does the cast survive into the `.pr` so runtime can use it for things like format coercion later? My read: **pure compile-time for now**, can persist later if a use case emerges. Don't widen the contract before there is a consumer.
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text: Per-action `Build()` method captured as section A.6 (deferred) and as open-note #8. The plan ships LLM-only inference for now (per-action prompt rules in A.4, plus the `(type)` hint convention in A.5); the `Build()` method generalizes those into actual code on its own future branch. Will add a `todos.md` entry for it. Good idea — it's the natural place for the path-parsing logic and would replace prompt-language inference with something testable.

plan.md.text:
just want to note, not all are written to action variable.set, here is exmple 

'- read file.csv, where zip=101, write to %list%'

read.file | list.where | variable.set
plan.md.anchor: > When a variable reference or string literal is followed by `(type)` in an action argument, treat `type` as a PLang type hint for the slot. If the action would emit a trailing `variable.set` for that arg's data, use the hint as the Type. Unknown type names should still be passed through — coercion happens at runtime, where `variable.set` will error if it can't convert.
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text: [PlangType("report")] are we really using this attribute? I feel like the type should come from the class otherwise we could have drift
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text: Agreed — dropped from `Report`. The PLang type name should derive from the class name (lowercased) by default. The attribute does have a legitimate use when the class name would collide with a module name (e.g. `app/tester/File.cs` uses `[PlangType("testfile")]` because bare `file` would clash with the `file` module), but `Report` has no collision so no attribute. Filed as open-note #6: source generator should derive by default, attribute becomes an explicit override on collision only. CLAUDE.md proposal worth writing on this branch.
plan.md.author: architect
